setup.context: At the school snack table with your coins

root.narration: You want to buy a juice box that costs one dollar. You have a bunch of coins in your pocket: four quarters, some dimes, and some nickels. The snack helper is waiting. What do you do?

choices.text: Count out four quarters because you know four quarters equal one dollar
Dump all your coins on the table and ask the helper to pick out the right amount
Hand over one nickel because it is the biggest coin you have

outcome.headline: Perfect payment!
outcome.explanation: You knew that four quarters make one dollar, so you counted them out and paid the exact amount. The helper smiled and handed you your juice box right away.
outcome.whatToRemember: Four quarters make one dollar. Ten dimes also make one dollar. Learning how coins add up helps you pay for things all by yourself.

keyTerms.definition: A coin worth 25 cents. Four quarters equal one dollar.

outcome.headline: You got your juice, but needed help
outcome.explanation: The helper picked out the right coins for you and you got your juice box. But next time you might not have someone to help you count.
outcome.whatToRemember: Practice counting coins at home so you can pay for things on your own. It feels great to do it yourself!

outcome.headline: Not enough money!
outcome.explanation: A nickel is only worth five cents, not one dollar. Even though it is a big coin, it is not worth as much as a quarter. You did not have enough to buy the juice box.
outcome.whatToRemember: The size of a coin does not tell you how much it is worth. A nickel is bigger than a dime, but a dime is worth more. Learn what each coin is worth!
